01-01: A new year, a fresh page. As the saying goes: well begun is half done.
01-02: Deep midwinter in Rockland County: the river quiet, the kettle busy.
01-03: Proverb: a guest is God, atithi devo bhava. Call someone today.
01-04: On this day in 1643, Isaac Newton was born: by the calendar he'd have used.
01-05: The days are already growing longer, a minute or two at a time.
01-06: Little by little, the camel goes into the pot of greens: slow progress is still progress.
01-07: A good day for soup. Almost any day is a good day for soup.
01-08: On this day in 1935, Elvis Presley was born in Tupelo, Mississippi.
01-09: Proverb: drop by drop fills the lake.
01-10: January's full moon is called the Wolf Moon in old farmers' almanacs.
01-11: Winter wisdom: the warmest blanket is a long phone call with an old friend.
01-12: Swami Vivekananda's birthday (1863): celebrated in India as National Youth Day.
01-13: Lohri season in Punjab: bonfires, sesame sweets, and song.
01-14: Makar Sankranti: the sun turns north, and kites fill the skies of Gujarat.
01-15: Martin Luther King Jr. was born on this day in 1929.
01-16: Proverb: even a journey of a thousand miles begins beneath one's feet.
01-17: Benjamin Franklin, printer of almanacs like this one, was born this day in 1706.
01-18: Snow on the cedars along the Palisades: worth a look out the window.
01-19: Tea tastes best when shared. Second-best: with a biscuit.
01-20: Every four years, a new American president is sworn in on this date.
01-21: Proverb: the tongue has no bones, yet it can break a heart, speak gently.
01-22: Midwinter note: the chickadees at the feeder are braver than they look.
01-23: Good handwriting day: write a letter; the postage stamp still works wonders.
01-24: Gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill, California, on this day in 1848.
01-25: Robert Burns Night in Scotland: poetry read aloud after supper.
01-26: Republic Day of India: the constitution took effect this day in 1950.
01-27: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart was born on this day in 1756.
01-28: Proverb: knowledge is a treasure no thief can touch.
01-29: The seed catalogs arrive in January for a reason: spring is a promise.
01-30: Mahatma Gandhi is remembered today with prayers for peace.
01-31: The month ends; the light at five o'clock says so.
02-01: February begins: the shortest month, often the bravest.
02-02: Groundhog Day: Punxsutawney Phil makes his famous forecast.
02-03: Proverb: a stitch in time saves nine.
02-04: Rosa Parks was born on this day in 1913.
02-05: The cardinals look their reddest against February snow.
02-06: Babe Ruth was born on this day in 1895.
02-07: Proverb: patience is bitter, but its fruit is sweet.
02-08: A good day to sort the photograph drawer: and to telephone about what you find.
02-09: The Beatles first appeared on the Ed Sullivan Show this day in 1964.
02-10: February's full moon is the Snow Moon in the old almanacs.
02-11: Thomas Edison was born on this day in 1847.
02-12: Abraham Lincoln was born on this day in 1809.
02-13: Eve of Valentine's: a fine evening to dig out the good stationery.
02-14: Valentine's Day: love, in any language, needs no translation.
02-15: Proverb: a single rose can be a garden; a single friend, a world.
02-16: Snowdrops are stirring under the leaf litter, whatever the thermometer says.
02-17: Random Acts of Kindness Day: pass one along.
02-18: Proverb: the heart at rest sees a feast in everything.
02-19: Copernicus, who set the sun at the center, was born this day in 1473.
02-20: The light is noticeably longer now: past five-thirty and holding.
02-21: Proverb: when an elder speaks, a library opens.
02-22: George Washington was born on this day in 1732.
02-23: Basant season in north India: the mustard fields turn yellow.
02-24: A good day for jalebi. There is rarely a bad one.
02-25: Proverb: slow and steady wins the race.
02-26: Maple sap begins to run in the Hudson Valley about now.
02-27: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow, poet of 'Paul Revere's Ride,' born this day in 1807.
02-28: February closes its books: neat, brief, and businesslike.
02-29: Leap Day! The calendar's once-in-four-years bonus: spend it well.
03-01: March comes in like a lion, the saying goes. We shall see.
03-02: Dr. Seuss was born on this day in 1904.
03-03: Proverb: a book is a garden carried in the pocket.
03-04: On this day in 1789, the U.S. Constitution went into effect.
03-05: Crocuses at the south wall: spring's first punctuation.
03-06: Michelangelo was born on this day in 1475.
03-07: Proverb: the best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago; the second-best is today.
03-08: International Women's Day: to the women who hold communities together.
03-09: The red-winged blackbirds are back in the marshes: listen for them.
03-10: Holika bonfire season approaches: stories of good over mischief.
03-11: Proverb: God gave us two ears and one mouth, in that proportion for a reason.
03-12: Juliette Gordon Low founded the Girl Scouts on this day in 1912.
03-13: March's full moon is the Worm Moon: the ground is waking.
03-14: Pi Day (3.14): mathematicians celebrate with actual pie. Albert Einstein was born today, 1879.
03-15: The Ides of March: beware, said the soothsayer; have a nice day, say we.
03-16: Proverb: a clear conscience is the softest pillow.
03-17: St. Patrick's Day: everyone is a little Irish today.
03-18: Time to start tomato seeds on the windowsill in Rockland County.
03-19: The swallows traditionally return to Capistrano on this day.
03-20: Around today, the spring equinox: day and night in perfect balance.
03-21: World Poetry Day: recite one from memory; you still know it.
03-22: Proverb: truth has a beautiful face and needs no jewelry.
03-23: The forsythia is about to do its yellow trumpet act.
03-24: Harry Houdini was born on this day in 1874.
03-25: Proverb: he who plants a garden plants happiness.
03-26: Robert Frost, poet of stone walls and snowy woods, born this day in 1874.
03-27: A walk after lunch adds flavor to the afternoon.
03-28: Proverb: a kind word warms three winter months.
03-29: Baseball's opening days draw near: oil the radio.
03-30: Vincent van Gogh was born on this day in 1853.
03-31: March goes out: lion or lamb, it kept things interesting.
04-01: April Fools' Day: believe half of what you read today, including this.
04-02: Hans Christian Andersen, teller of tales, born this day in 1805.
04-03: Proverb: little drops of water make the mighty ocean.
04-04: Maya Angelou was born on this day in 1928.
04-05: The peepers are singing in every wet corner of the county.
04-06: The modern Olympic Games first opened on this day in 1896, in Athens.
04-07: World Health Day: take the stairs, then take a rest. Both count.
04-08: Proverb: a tree is known by its fruit, not by its leaves.
04-09: Ramanavami season: the story of Rama told once more, sweetly.
04-10: Daffodils along Route 45: April's official signature.
04-11: Proverb: when the character is good, the home is happy.
04-12: Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on this day in 1961.
04-13: Vaisakhi: harvest festival of Punjab, drums and new wheat.
04-14: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar, father of India's constitution, born this day in 1891.
04-15: Leonardo da Vinci was born on this day in 1452.
04-16: Charlie Chaplin was born on this day in 1889.
04-17: Proverb: a closed mouth catches no flies, but an open one tastes the spring.
04-18: Paul Revere rode on this night in 1775.
04-19: The shad are running the Hudson: an old river tradition.
04-20: Proverb: rain in April is worth a cart of gold.
04-21: Queen Elizabeth II was born on this day in 1926.
04-22: Earth Day: first celebrated in 1970. The planet appreciates the card.
04-23: William Shakespeare's birthday is kept on this day (1564).
04-24: The lilacs are budding: sniff-testing season begins shortly.
04-25: Proverb: one flower does not make a garland.
04-26: John James Audubon, painter of birds, born this day in 1785.
04-27: A good day to clean the teapot properly. You know the one.
04-28: Proverb: where there is tea, there is hope.
04-29: Duke Ellington was born on this day in 1899.
04-30: April signs off in green ink.
05-01: May Day: baskets of flowers on doorknobs, by the old custom.
05-02: Satyajit Ray, the great filmmaker of Bengal, born this day in 1921.
05-03: The orioles return for the orange halves about now.
05-04: Proverb: a friend's eye is a good mirror.
05-05: Cinco de Mayo: and Rabindranath Tagore's birthday week begins.
05-06: Proverb: do good and throw it in the river, kindness needs no receipt.
05-07: Rabindranath Tagore, Nobel laureate poet, born this day in 1861.
05-08: The first hummingbird of the season is due any minute. Sugar water ready?
05-09: Proverb: mother's blessings reach where the eye cannot.
05-10: The transcontinental railroad was joined with a golden spike this day in 1869.
05-11: Lilac time in the Hudson Valley: the air does half the talking.
05-12: Florence Nightingale was born on this day in 1820: Nurses Day.
05-13: Proverb: a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.
05-14: Warbler migration peaks: small birds, long journeys, no luggage.
05-15: Safe now to set out the tomato plants in Rockland County.
05-16: Proverb: sweetness of speech multiplies friends.
05-17: A good evening for sitting on the stoop. Take the radio.
05-18: Proverb: the world is one family, vasudhaiva kutumbakam.
05-19: The peonies are staging their annual opera.
05-20: Charles Lindbergh took off for Paris on this day in 1927.
05-21: Proverb: he who has health has hope, and he who has hope has everything.
05-22: Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, creator of Sherlock Holmes, born this day in 1859.
05-24: The Brooklyn Bridge opened on this day in 1883.
05-25: Proverb: courtesy costs nothing and buys everything.
05-26: Memorial Day week: flags out along every porch in the county.
05-27: The Golden Gate Bridge opened on this day in 1937.
05-28: Proverb: a single conversation with a wise person is worth a month's study.
05-29: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay reached Everest's summit this day in 1953.
05-30: Peony petals on the sidewalk: May's confetti.
05-31: Walt Whitman, poet of these very riverbanks, born this day in 1819.
06-01: June begins: the year's most generous month, ask any rose.
06-02: Proverb: a contented mind is a continual feast.
06-03: The first fireflies are due in the yards of Pomona any evening now.
06-04: Proverb: speak sweetly, words are the dress of thought.
06-05: World Environment Day: plant something, even basil counts.
06-06: On this day in 1944, the Allies landed at Normandy: D-Day.
06-07: Strawberry season opens at the farm stands.
06-08: Frank Lloyd Wright, architect, born this day in 1867.
06-09: Proverb: the roots of education are bitter, but the fruit is sweet.
06-10: Judy Garland was born on this day in 1922: somewhere over the rainbow.
06-11: Jacques Cousteau, explorer of the sea, born this day in 1910.
06-12: Anne Frank received her famous diary on this day in 1942.
06-13: Proverb: one hand cannot clap, togetherness makes the sound.
06-14: Flag Day: the Stars and Stripes adopted this day in 1777.
06-15: June's full moon is the Strawberry Moon. Aptly named, deliciously timed.
06-16: Proverb: a mango shared tastes twice as sweet.
06-17: The catbirds are imitating everyone again. No respect.
06-18: Proverb: he who asks is a fool for five minutes; he who does not stays one forever.
06-19: Juneteenth: freedom's news reached Texas on this day in 1865.
06-20: Around today, the summer solstice: the year's longest light.
06-21: International Yoga Day: the world stretches together, as we do on Mondays.
06-23: The zucchini has begun. Lock nothing; you will receive some anyway.
06-24: Midsummer: in old Europe, bonfires; in Rockland, barbecue smoke.
06-25: Proverb: better an honest no than a dishonest yes.
06-26: Pearl S. Buck, who wrote between two worlds, born this day in 1892.
06-27: Helen Keller was born on this day in 1880.
06-28: Proverb: comparisons are the thief of joy, your garden is lovely as it is.
06-29: The roadside chicory opens its blue shop early and closes by noon.
06-30: Half the year done. The ledger looks fine; carry on.
07-01: July opens with sweet corn rumors at the farm stands.
07-02: Thurgood Marshall, the first Black Supreme Court justice, born this day in 1908.
07-03: Proverb: a watched pot never boils, walk away, it knows.
07-04: Independence Day: the Declaration was adopted this day in 1776.
07-05: Leftover potato salad: arguably the true national dish of July 5th.
07-06: The Dalai Lama was born on this day in 1935.
07-07: Proverb: the deeper the well, the sweeter the water.
07-08: Hydrangea season along every porch in the county.
07-09: Proverb: a borrowed coat never keeps one warm.
07-10: Nikola Tesla was born on this day in 1856.
07-11: July's full moon is the Buck Moon in the old almanacs.
07-12: Henry David Thoreau, keeper of Walden Pond, born this day in 1817.
07-13: Proverb: when the mango is ripe, it falls by itself, some things cannot be hurried.
07-14: Bastille Day: France's national day of liberty.
07-15: Rembrandt was born on this day in 1606.
07-16: Apollo 11 lifted off for the moon on this day in 1969.
07-17: Proverb: a fan in winter, a quilt in summer, timing is everything.
07-18: Nelson Mandela was born on this day in 1918: Mandela Day.
07-19: The cicadas have started their long afternoon argument.
07-20: On this day in 1969, Apollo 11 landed and humans first walked on the moon.
07-21: Proverb: the boughs that bear most hang lowest, humility is strength.
07-22: Tomatoes worth bragging about begin arriving this week.
07-23: Proverb: after the rain, the sun; after sorrow, song.
07-24: Amelia Earhart was born on this day in 1897.
07-25: Sweet corn, butter, napkin. The holy trinity of late July.
07-26: Proverb: a wise enemy is better than a foolish friend.
07-27: The Korean War armistice was signed on this day in 1953: peace remembered.
07-28: Beatrix Potter, mother of Peter Rabbit, born this day in 1866.
07-29: Proverb: it takes a whole village to raise a child, and to run a good schedule.
07-30: Henry Ford was born on this day in 1863.
08-01: August: the month that tastes of peaches.
08-02: Proverb: wherever you go, go with all your heart.
08-03: The goldfinches are nesting late, as is their cheerful habit.
08-04: Louis Armstrong was born on this day in 1901. What a wonderful world.
08-05: Proverb: a good neighbor is a found treasure.
08-06: Lucille Ball was born on this day in 1911.
08-07: Purple loosestrife lights up the wet meadows: lovely, if bossy.
08-08: Proverb: even sandalwood must be rubbed to give fragrance, effort releases sweetness.
08-09: August's full moon is the Sturgeon Moon, named for our own river fish.
08-10: The Smithsonian Institution was founded on this day in 1846.
08-11: Proverb: where there is love, there is no darkness.
08-12: The Perseid meteor shower peaks about now: look up after ten.
08-13: Alfred Hitchcock was born on this day in 1899.
08-14: Eve of India's Independence: flags pressed, sweets ordered.
08-15: India's Independence Day: freedom at midnight, 1947. Jai Hind.
08-16: Proverb: a tree laden with fruit bends low.
08-17: Raksha Bandhan season: threads of protection between brothers and sisters.
08-18: Proverb: the lamp does not speak; its light introduces it.
08-19: Orville Wright was born on this day in 1871: National Aviation Day.
08-20: Tomato sandwiches at their constitutional peak this week.
08-21: Proverb: count your blessings on your fingers, your troubles on none.
08-22: Krishna Janmashtami season: butter guarded, flutes everywhere.
08-23: The first hint of gold in the sugar maples, if you squint.
08-24: Proverb: a book read twice is two books.
08-25: The National Park Service was founded on this day in 1916.
08-26: Women's Equality Day: the 19th Amendment was certified this day in 1920.
08-27: Mother Teresa was born on this day in 1910.
08-28: Dr. King delivered 'I Have a Dream' on this day in 1963.
08-29: Proverb: the night is darkest before the dawn.
08-30: School buses are practicing their routes: summer checks its watch.
08-31: August closes the screen door gently behind itself.
09-01: September: the year's second New Year, ask any student.
09-02: Proverb: a fall into a ditch makes you wiser.
09-03: The monarch butterflies begin their long commute to Mexico.
09-04: Proverb: rice and blessings multiply when shared.
09-05: Teachers' Day in India: honoring Dr. Radhakrishnan, born this day in 1888.
09-06: Goldenrod season: the fields wear marigold, our favorite color.
09-07: Grandma Moses, who began painting at 78, was born this day in 1860.
09-08: Proverb: it is never too late to be what you might have been.
09-09: Apple-picking opens across the Hudson Valley.
09-10: Ganesh Chaturthi season: fresh starts and modak sweets.
09-11: A day of remembrance: call someone you love today.
09-12: Proverb: the heaviest rains fall on the roof that is already mended.
09-13: Roald Dahl, teller of tall tales, born this day in 1916.
09-14: September's full moon is often the Harvest Moon: big as a dinner plate.
09-15: Proverb: a good word is an easy obligation.
09-16: The hummingbirds top off the tank and head south about now.
09-17: The U.S. Constitution was signed on this day in 1787.
09-18: Proverb: hurry is the wind that blows out the lamp.
09-20: Proverb: stretch your feet only as far as your blanket, and nap accordingly.
09-21: International Day of Peace: shanti, shanti, shanti.
09-22: Around today, the autumn equinox: the light splits the bill evenly.
09-23: The first sweater of the season makes its annual debut.
09-24: Jim Henson, father of the Muppets, born this day in 1936.
09-25: Proverb: a cracked pot still waters the flowers on the path.
09-26: Johnny Appleseed's birthday (1774): fitting, in apple country.
09-27: Navaratri season approaches: nine nights of dance and devotion.
09-28: Proverb: the world steps aside for one who knows where they are going.
09-29: Gene Autry recorded his cowboy classics; the radio remembers this week.
09-30: September files its papers: foliage report due next month.
10-01: October: the maples begin their famous farewell tour.
10-02: Gandhi Jayanti: Mahatma Gandhi was born this day in 1869.
10-03: Proverb: a lamp lights another lamp and loses nothing.
10-04: Sputnik launched on this day in 1957: the space age began.
10-05: World Teachers' Day: thank the one you still quote.
10-06: Peak foliage forecast for Rockland: any week now, and worth the wait.
10-07: Proverb: don't judge a day by its weather, nor a book by its cover.
10-08: The woolly bear caterpillars are crossing roads with great purpose.
10-09: Proverb: he who sows kindness reaps community.
10-10: October's full moon is the Hunter's Moon in the old almanacs.
10-11: Eleanor Roosevelt was born on this day in 1884.
10-12: Proverb: a guest brings blessings with their footsteps.
10-13: Cider doughnut season reaches its solemn peak.
10-14: Dussehra season: good prevails; effigies, fireworks, and sweets.
10-15: Dr. A. P. J. Abdul Kalam, India's beloved 'people's president,' born this day in 1931.
10-16: Proverb: hunger is the best chutney.
10-17: The first frost usually visits Rockland County about now: cover the basil.
10-18: Proverb: a patient person eats ripe fruit.
10-19: The geese are practicing their V's overhead: listen at dusk.
10-20: Proverb: even the moon has spots, perfection is not required.
10-21: Alfred Nobel, founder of the Nobel Prizes, born this day in 1833.
10-22: Rake, rest, repeat. The leaves are winning, gracefully.
10-23: Proverb: where the needle goes, the thread follows.
10-24: United Nations Day: the UN charter took effect this day in 1945.
10-25: Pablo Picasso was born on this day in 1881.
10-26: Diwali season nears: clean the lamps, ready the wicks.
10-27: Theodore Roosevelt was born on this day in 1858.
10-28: The Statue of Liberty was dedicated on this day in 1886.
10-29: Proverb: a small lamp can do what the great sun cannot, shine at night.
10-30: Pumpkins on every stoop, each grinning at its own joke.
10-31: Halloween: keep good candy by the door and a better story by the fire.
11-01: November opens its quiet ledger: gratitude entries accepted daily.
11-02: Proverb: gratitude turns what we have into enough.
11-03: The last leaves hang on like encore performers.
11-04: Proverb: a well is not dug after the house catches fire, prepare in peace.
11-05: Diwali falls in this season many years: the festival of lights, our namesake joy.
11-06: Proverb: the lamp you light for another brightens your own path first.
11-07: Marie Curie, twice a Nobel laureate, born this day in 1867.
11-08: The juncos arrive from Canada: winter's polite advance party.
11-09: The Berlin Wall opened on this day in 1989.
11-10: Proverb: warm words make a warm house.
11-11: Veterans Day: the Armistice was signed this day in 1918. Honor and thanks.
11-12: Proverb: the thread of kinship stretches but does not break.
11-13: Robert Louis Stevenson, author of Treasure Island, born this day in 1850.
11-14: Children's Day in India: Jawaharlal Nehru, born this day in 1889, loved roses and children.
11-15: November's full moon is the Beaver Moon in the old almanacs.
11-16: Proverb: tolerance is the fragrance flowers give when trampled.
11-17: Soup weather, officially. The big pot earns its keep this month.
11-18: Mickey Mouse made his debut on this day in 1928.
11-19: Lincoln delivered the Gettysburg Address on this day in 1863.
11-20: Proverb: four eyes see more than two, ask before you decide.
11-21: World Television Day: and still nothing on. Read instead.
11-22: Proverb: a full cup must be carried steadily.
11-23: Thanksgiving week: the kitchen becomes the capital of the house.
11-24: Proverb: who shares their meal never eats alone.
11-25: Cranberry sauce: from a can or from scratch, the debate resumes annually.
11-26: Proverb: contentment is the greatest wealth.
11-27: Leftover sandwiches at their architectural finest this week.
11-28: Proverb: a tree does not withhold its shade even from the woodcutter.
11-29: Louisa May Alcott, author of Little Women, born this day in 1832.
11-30: Mark Twain was born on this day in 1835.
12-01: December: the month of lamps, in every tradition.
12-02: Proverb: a candle loses nothing by lighting another candle.
12-03: The first serious frost flowers appear on the windowpanes.
12-04: Proverb: the winter teaches the value of the quilt.
12-05: Walt Disney was born on this day in 1901.
12-06: St. Nicholas Day: shoes by the door in the old country style.
12-07: Pearl Harbor Remembrance Day: a moment of quiet for 1941.
12-08: Proverb: a snowflake never falls in the wrong place.
12-09: Cocoa season at full strength. Marshmallows: yes.
12-10: Nobel Prizes are awarded today, on Alfred Nobel's anniversary.
12-11: Proverb: words should be weighed, not counted.
12-12: Poinsettia Day: the holiday's official houseplant takes its bow.
12-13: St. Lucia Day: candles and saffron buns in the Scandinavian custom.
12-14: December's full moon is the Cold Moon. The almanacs do not exaggerate.
12-15: Proverb: he who burns wood in summer warms twice.
12-16: The Boston Tea Party took place on this day in 1773.
12-17: The Wright brothers first flew on this day in 1903: twelve seconds that changed everything.
12-18: Proverb: the work praises the worker.
12-19: Cards in the mail by today reach almost anywhere by Christmas.
12-20: Proverb: shared joy is double joy; shared sorrow is half sorrow.
12-21: Around today, the winter solstice: from here, the light only grows.
12-22: Srinivasa Ramanujan, the self-taught mathematical genius, born this day in 1887.
12-23: The fruitcake jokes begin. The fruitcake, unbothered, outlasts them all.
12-24: Christmas Eve: the quietest, brightest night of the calendar.
12-25: Merry Christmas: peace on earth, good will to all.
12-26: Boxing Day: leftovers, naps, and the year's best lazy afternoon.
12-27: Louis Pasteur was born on this day in 1822.
12-28: Proverb: the year is a book; write a kind last chapter.
12-29: Proverb: even the longest night ends in sunrise.
12-30: Time to find the new calendar and argue about where it hangs.
12-31: New Year's Eve: to old friends, new mornings, and the light of life. जीवन ज्योति!
